# ResearchHub — Full Reference

> ResearchHub is an open science platform where researchers publish, peer review, fund, and discuss scientific research. Contributors earn ResearchCoin (RSC) for their work.

## About ResearchHub

ResearchHub's mission is to accelerate the pace of scientific research. It operates as "a GitHub for science" — a collaborative platform for publishing preprints, conducting open peer review, funding research, and discussing findings in topic-specific communities called Hubs. The platform compensates accepted peer reviewers ($150 per review), publishes content open access, and completes peer review in as few as 14 days. User-contributed content (excluding peer reviews) is licensed under CC BY 4.0. Maintained by the ResearchHub Foundation and ResearchHub, Inc.

The platform is maintained by two entities:

- **ResearchHub, Inc.** — the technology company that builds and operates the platform, based in San Francisco, CA.
- **ResearchHub Foundation** — the nonprofit that funds research, manages the endowment, and oversees editorial programs including the ResearchHub Journal.

ResearchHub differs from traditional academic publishers because it compensates peer reviewers ($150 per accepted review), publishes content open access, completes peer review in as few as 14 days, and uses a token-based incentive system (ResearchCoin) to reward contributions. User-contributed content (excluding peer reviews) is licensed under CC BY 4.0; peer review content is governed by a separate license (see Terms of Service, Section 4A).

## Core Values

ResearchHub believes scientific research should be:

- **Accessible** — No paywalls, no costs to participate. Summaries in plain English.
- **Collaborative** — Academics and non-academics interact publicly, incentivized by ResearchCoin.
- **Efficient** — Traditional funding-to-publication cycles take 3–5 years. ResearchHub aims for at least one order of magnitude faster.

## Products & Services

### ResearchHub Journal

An open access, peer-reviewed scientific journal built for speed and transparency.

- Accepts research across all scientific disciplines.
- Peer review turnaround in as few as 14 days.
- Submissions are immediately available as preprints.
- Reviews are conducted openly (unblinded) and published alongside the paper.
- Reviewers are compensated $150 in RSC per accepted review.
- Papers that complete review are published as the version of record.

More: https://researchhub.com/journal

### Peer Review Program

ResearchHub compensates researchers for peer-reviewing scientific preprints. Unlike traditional peer review — typically unpaid and months-long — accepted reviews on ResearchHub are paid $150 each with turnaround around 10 days.

Reviewers build a public track record that strengthens their academic profile. Peer reviews provide context for authors, funders, and readers — they are not used to gatekeep publication.

Guidelines: https://docs.researchhub.com/researchhub-foundation/programs-and-initiatives/peer-review-program/peer-review-guidelines

More: https://researchhub.com/earn

### Research Funding

Direct funding pathways for scientific research:

- **Grants:** Open opportunities from the Foundation and community funders, with defined scope, funding amount in USD, and deadlines. Spans biomedical sciences, computational biology, physics, economics, and more.
- **Proposals:** Researchers submit their own proposals for original work. Each undergoes open peer review evaluating methodology, significance, and feasibility. Funded projects receive RSC directly.
- **Nonprofit attachment:** US-based researchers can attach their university nonprofit for tax-deductible contributions and fast fund delivery (typically 2–3 weeks).

More: https://researchhub.com/fund

### ResearchCoin (RSC)

ResearchCoin (RSC) is an ERC-20 token earned by contributing to ResearchHub.

Uses:

- Create bounties for peer reviews and specific tasks.
- Tip users for valuable contributions.
- Fund research proposals.

Documentation: https://docs.researchhub.com

### Hubs (Topics)

Research is organized into "Hubs" by discipline. Each Hub acts as a live journal, with highly upvoted content rising to the top. Users can browse, follow, and contribute to any Hub. Within each Hub, content can be viewed by Popular or Latest.

Browse: https://researchhub.com/browse

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Is content on ResearchHub open?

Yes. User-contributed content (excluding peer reviews) is shared under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 (CC BY 4.0). Anyone can reuse that content for any purpose with attribution — a hyperlink back to the source page is sufficient. Peer review content is governed by a separate license under the Terms of Service (Section 4A). CC BY is the gold standard for scholarly content, used by PLOS, eLife, and BMC.

### What papers can be uploaded?

Users can create a page for any paper to enable summary and discussion. Fulltext PDF uploads are limited to papers released under CC BY or CC0 licenses that are already publicly available elsewhere.

### How does peer review work?

Researchers browse open peer-review bounties, find papers in their field, and submit structured reviews following published guidelines. Reviews are evaluated by the requesting author or editorial team. Approved reviews earn $150 in RSC and are published openly alongside the paper.

### How does research funding work?

Researchers apply to open grants or create their own proposals. Proposals undergo open peer review. Funded projects receive RSC directly. US-based researchers can attach a university nonprofit for tax-deductible contributions.

### Who created ResearchHub?

A team based in San Francisco, CA. The idea was initially proposed by Brian Armstrong. The platform is maintained through a partnership between the ResearchHub Foundation and ResearchHub, Inc.
